title: From Portobello Road to a houseboat — twenty-five years and what I am proud of.
excerpt: I started with a market stall on Portobello Road. At 23 I got my first £45,000 order. I made jewellery in my bedroom while a buyer at Reiss put me into 50,000 pieces a week.
body:
I started with a market stall on Portobello Road. I was nineteen. I had no money, no contacts, and no plan beyond a feeling that I could make something people wanted to wear.

At twenty-three I got my first £45,000 order. I remember opening the email. I remember not telling anyone for two days because I needed to be sure it was real. I flew to China and India to source materials I could not afford. I learned how to negotiate in rooms where I was the only woman. I made every piece by hand in my bedroom while the first buyer at Reiss put my jewellery into 50,000 pieces a week.

The stockists came after that. Harrods. Selfridges. Liberty. Harvey Nichols. Isetan and Hankyu in Tokyo. Henri Bendel in New York. Each one a door I had walked into not knowing whether it would open.

Then everything I had built came apart at the same time. The marriage. The business as I had run it. The eldest child I lost contact with for reasons that took me years to understand. I moved onto a houseboat on Taggs Island because that was what was available and because something in me knew I needed water near me.

I rebuilt. Not the same business. A different one. The jewellery now is made in the UK because ethics matter to me more than margin. The coaching practice exists because women kept asking me how I had survived what I had survived. The houseboat is still home.

What I am proud of: the children who came back. The work that is honest. The fact that nothing about this trajectory was inevitable, and I kept going anyway.

Twenty-five years. Still here.

title: The Violet Light Technique — the daily visualisation I give every client.
excerpt: The Violet Light Technique is the simplest practice I teach. Five minutes. No equipment. No belief required. Just intention and a colour your nervous system already responds to.
body:
The Violet Light Technique is the simplest practice I teach. Five minutes. No equipment. No belief required. Just intention and a colour your nervous system already responds to.

Why violet. The colour sits at the highest visible frequency of light. Spiritual traditions across the world associate it with transformation and clearing. Whether or not you accept the spiritual framing, the practice works because intentional visualisation reliably shifts nervous system state. The colour is the anchor.

The practice. Find a chair. Close your eyes. Imagine a soft violet light beginning at the crown of your head and slowly moving down through your body. As it passes through, it dissolves anything that is not yours to carry. Other people's stress. Today's residue. The thing the colleague said that you have not let go of. Let it move all the way through to your feet and into the ground.

Do this for five minutes. Once a day, ideally morning. More if the day is heavy.

What you will notice. The first week, possibly nothing dramatic. By the second week, a subtle but consistent shift in how easily things settle. By the third week, you reach for it the way you might reach for water when you are thirsty.

This is the foundational daily practice in my 3-month coaching programme. It is also a perfectly complete practice on its own. Start with it. Build everything else around it.

title: Trauma Release Exercises — what they are, what they cost, and why I trained in them.
excerpt: TRE is a six-exercise protocol developed by Dr David Berceli that releases stored tension from the psoas — the deep muscle where your body holds trauma.
body:
TRE — Trauma Release Exercises — is a six-exercise protocol developed by Dr David Berceli that releases stored tension from the psoas. The psoas is the deep muscle that connects your spine to your legs. It is where the body holds trauma. Not metaphorically. Physically.

When something overwhelming happens that you cannot fully process, the body stores it in the muscle tissue. The psoas tightens and locks. Over years, this becomes chronic tension you stop noticing because it has been there for so long it feels like baseline.

TRE works because it triggers the body's natural tremoring response — the same response you see in animals after a near-death encounter, where they shake for several minutes and then walk away as though nothing happened. Humans have suppressed this response. TRE restores it.

What a session looks like. Ninety minutes, conducted remotely or in person. The six exercises are gentle physical positions that fatigue specific muscle groups. After the exercises, the body begins to tremor on its own. You lie down and let it happen. It is not painful. It is not dramatic. It is the body finally completing something it began years ago.

Most people feel results in one or two sessions. Some need more. The price is £75 per session.

I trained in TRE because I had ten years of stored trauma from a devastating divorce that no amount of therapy was shifting. Talking about it kept me in it. TRE moved it through. That is what made me become a coach.

The work I do now is built on this principle. The body knows. You just have to let it speak.

title: Kirra Kirra and Narc Abuse Aid — the projects I am building beyond the coaching.
excerpt: Kirra Kirra means Sparkle Sparkle in Japanese. It is an animated children's show I am developing about mental health for kids. Narc Abuse Aid is the charity I am building alongside it.
body:
Kirra Kirra means Sparkle Sparkle in Japanese. It is an animated children's show I am developing about mental health for kids — the kind of show I would have wanted my daughters to watch when they were small. Where feelings have names. Where the brave thing is to say what is happening. Where the magic is in the truth.

The wellness industry talks endlessly about adult healing and almost never about prevention. Children who grow up with the language of nervous system regulation, emotional honesty, and self-knowledge become adults who do not need to spend their forties reverse-engineering what their childhood did not teach them. Kirra Kirra is for them.

Narc Abuse Aid is the charity I am building alongside it. There is currently no UK-specific organisation that supports survivors of narcissistic abuse with the specific recovery resources they need. Most general domestic abuse charities are extraordinary but their remit is broader. Survivors of coercive control and narcissistic dynamics need targeted support — the recognition that what happened was real, the language for what they survived, and access to the recovery work that actually moves the needle on the nervous system damage they are carrying.

Narc Abuse Aid will fund access to recovery coaching, trauma-informed therapy, and somatic work for survivors who could not otherwise afford it.

Both projects are slow builds. Both are necessary. Both will outlast me, which is the point.
